title: Who Was Paul (Rav Shaul)?
description: A guided study path exploring the historical, Jewish, theological, and apostolic identity of Rav Shaul (Paul).

steps.title: Historical Identity
steps.summary: Who Paul was historically, ethnically, politically, and geographically.
reflection: Why does Paul's Jewish identity matter?
Why is Roman citizenship significant?

steps.title: The Apostolic World Was Jewish
steps.summary: The earliest followers of Yeshua emerged from within Second Temple Judaism, not from a separate religion called Christianity.
reflection: Did the earliest believers think they were leaving Judaism?
Why does Paul continue identifying as a Pharisee?
How does first-century context change the way we hear Paul?

steps.title: Education and Formation
steps.summary: Paul's Pharisaic background and education under Gamaliel.
reflection: Why is Gamaliel important?
Would an untrained outsider speak the way Paul speaks?

steps.title: Before Following Yeshua
steps.summary: Understanding Paul's original beliefs and zeal.
reflection: Why did Paul oppose the early Jesus movement?
What did covenant fidelity mean to him?

steps.title: The Damascus Experience
steps.summary: Paul's encounter with the risen Messiah.
reflection: Did Paul describe this as leaving Judaism?
How did this event reinterpret Israel's story?

steps.title: Mission to the Gentiles
steps.summary: Paul's work among the nations and the formation of assemblies.
reflection: What were ekklesia communities?
Why focus on Gentiles?

steps.title: Central Theological Ideas
steps.summary: Messiah, Torah, faithfulness, and covenant inclusion.
reflection: What does pistis really mean?
Was Paul anti-Torah?

steps.title: Major Misunderstandings
reflection: Did Paul create a new religion?
How have modern readers misunderstood him?

steps.title: Identity Tension
steps.summary: The many identities Paul held simultaneously.
reflection: Why is Paul difficult to interpret?
How do all his identities interact?

steps.title: Death and Legacy
reflection: Why did Paul's writings survive so prominently?
What legacy did he leave behind?
